description: Implement a `wreck-it export-graph` command that serialises the current task dependency graph as a Mermaid flowchart or Graphviz DOT file, showing task phases, dependency edges, status colours (pending/inprogress/completed/failed), artefact flows, and fan-out sub-task clusters. Auto-embed the generated diagram into a `WORKFLOW.md` file that is updated at the end of each loop iteration via a recurring evaluator-role task. This transforms wreck-it's internal DAG representation into living documentation that stakeholders can read without CLI access, directly leveraging the existing phase/depends_on/parent_id data model.

Build an intelligent model-routing layer that assigns the cheapest LLM tier capable of completing a task based on its complexity score, role, and historical success rates. Low-complexity (1–3) tasks are routed to fast/cheap models (e.g., gpt-4o-mini, claude-haiku); medium-complexity (4–6) tasks to mid-tier; high-complexity (7–10) tasks to flagship models. After a model fails, automatically escalate to the next tier and record the escalation in per-task agent memory for future routing decisions. Expose a `--model-routing` flag and a `[model_routing]` config section. This directly builds on the existing complexity field, cost_tracker, and multi-provider model support to cut average run costs by routing simple tasks cheaply.

Implement an adversarial debate review pattern for high-complexity tasks: before committing, spawn a second 'devil's advocate' agent that receives the git diff and critiques the implementation from an opposing perspective (security risks, alternative approaches, edge cases). The original implementer agent then rebuts the critique. A judge-role agent scores both arguments and decides whether to commit or retry with the combined feedback. Integrate as an optional task-level field `debate: true` that triggers this three-way exchange in the reflection loop. This extends the existing critic-actor reflection loop with a structured multi-agent debate pattern inspired by debate-as-alignment research.

Add a `wreck-it query '<question>'` sub-command that translates a natural-language question into a structured query over the task graph, artefact store, provenance audit trail, and agent memory files. Examples: 'which tasks failed more than twice?', 'what code did the auth task modify?', 'how much did last week's run cost?'. The command sends the question plus a schema description of the available data to the LLM, receives a JSON filter/aggregation plan, executes it locally, and prints a human-readable result. This surfaces wreck-it's rich structured data to users who prefer prose over CLI flags, uniquely combining the provenance trail and artefact system with LLM-powered query translation.

Add an optional sandbox execution mode where each agent task runs inside an ephemeral Docker container (or a Firecracker microVM via a configurable backend). The container is seeded with a snapshot of the work directory, runs the agent's code edits and tests in isolation, and only merges changes back to the host workspace if tests pass. Configure via `sandbox = 'docker'` in config. This addresses the growing concern about autonomous agents making irreversible changes to a live codebase, and aligns with the 'minimal footprint' principle emerging in agentic safety research. Integrates with the existing test runner and commit pipeline.

Implement a recurring `architecture_drift` evaluator-role task that reads a declarative architecture manifest (`.wreck-it/architecture.md` or `.wreck-it/architecture.toml`) describing intended module boundaries, dependency rules, and naming conventions. Each iteration, it runs static analysis on the actual codebase (using treesitter or regex-based heuristics) to detect violations — e.g., a module importing from a layer it should not, or a file placed in the wrong directory. Detected violations are written as artefacts and trigger automatically generated remediation tasks. This uniquely combines wreck-it's recurring task cooldown, artefact chaining, and dynamic task generation into an automated architectural governance loop.

Extend wreck-it's existing webhook notification system into a first-class multi-channel notification hub supporting Slack (Block Kit messages), Discord (embeds), Microsoft Teams (Adaptive Cards), and email (SMTP). Notifications include task status transitions, loop completion summaries, budget threshold warnings, and failure reports with git diff links. Each channel is configured independently under `[[notifiers]]` in config.toml with per-channel event filters. This builds on the existing `--notify-webhook` infrastructure and replaces it with a typed, pluggable notifier trait, making wreck-it composable with the communication stacks teams already use.

Build a task effort estimation subsystem that, before each task execution, predicts its likely token cost, wall-clock duration, and probability of first-attempt success using a local regression model trained on historical execution data stored in the provenance trail. Predictions are surfaced in the TUI (as a 'forecast' column) and used by the scheduler to reorder the queue when budget is constrained. After each task completes, actual vs. predicted values are logged back into a rolling training dataset. The local model starts with a simple linear regressor over complexity/role/model features and can be upgraded to a neural model as data accumulates. This uniquely leverages wreck-it's provenance audit trail as a training dataset for meta-learning about agent behaviour.

Implement a `wreck-it replay <task-id>` command that reconstructs and re-runs a previously executed task in a read-only trace mode: it replays the exact prompt, codebase snapshot (via git at the commit before the task ran), and recorded LLM responses from the provenance audit trail — without consuming new LLM tokens or making new changes. The replay produces a structured diff between the original agent output and an optional new run (with a `--live` flag for fresh execution) to compare behaviour. This transforms the provenance audit trail from a passive log into an interactive debugging tool, enabling developers to diagnose why an agent produced unexpected output and iterate on prompt improvements offline.

Build a community template registry that allows users to publish, discover, and install wreck-it task template collections from a central index (hosted at wreckit.app or a GitHub-backed registry). Extend the existing `wreck-it template list/apply` commands with `template publish` (packages and uploads a local template set) and `template search <query>` (queries the registry). Templates are versioned, signed, and include metadata (domain, language, author, download count). Locally installed templates are cached in `~/.wreck-it/templates/`. This leverages the existing template system and extends the wreck-it ecosystem via a community flywheel — similar to how Helm charts or Terraform modules accelerated adoption of their respective tools.

Implement an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server mode for wreck-it so that AI assistants (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot Chat, Cursor) can directly query and manage the task pipeline via the standard MCP protocol. Expose MCP tools for: listing tasks and their statuses, adding new tasks, updating task status, reading artefacts, and triggering a loop iteration. This bridges wreck-it's structured task graph with the rapidly emerging MCP ecosystem, enabling humans to supervise autonomous loops through their preferred AI assistant interface rather than the CLI or TUI.

Add OpenTelemetry (OTEL) trace and metrics export to wreck-it's execution engine. Instrument task lifecycle events (start, complete, fail, retry) as OTEL spans with attributes for model name, token counts, task role, phase, and complexity. Export to configurable OTLP endpoints (Jaeger, Honeycomb, Grafana). This uniquely leverages wreck-it's existing cost tracking and provenance audit trail to provide deep observability into autonomous agent workflows — a capability no other agent framework offers out of the box.

Add a built-in security gate task kind that runs CodeQL, cargo-audit, or npm audit as a mandatory completion gate before any implementation task PR can be merged. Integrate into the task phase system so security scans run in a post-implementation phase automatically. Surface findings back to the implementing agent via the artefact system so it can self-remediate. This combines wreck-it's phase-based scheduling, artefact chaining, and retry loop into a first-class security posture — addressing the growing trend of shift-left security in AI-native development.

Implement a test coverage enforcer as a built-in evaluator-role task that automatically runs after each implementation task completes. The enforcer reads the coverage report artefact produced by the implementation phase, verifies that coverage did not regress, and either approves the task or triggers a retry with a coverage-improvement prompt. Use wreck-it's `inputs` artefact system to pass coverage data between phases. This creates a unique test-driven autonomous development loop where the AI is held accountable to coverage thresholds without human intervention.

Build an adaptive prompt optimization system that tracks which task description patterns correlate with successful first-attempt completions vs. repeated failures. After each failed attempt, analyze the task description and failure log using an ideas-role agent to suggest a rewritten task description. Store the rewrite in per-task agent memory and apply it on next retry. Surface aggregated pattern insights as a periodic report artefact. This uniquely leverages wreck-it's existing retry tracking, agent memory system, and artefact chaining to create a self-improving task specification loop.

Implement bidirectional sync between wreck-it tasks and external kanban systems (Linear, JIRA, Trello). When new tickets are created in the external system with a configurable label/tag, automatically generate wreck-it task objects and append them to the appropriate task file. When wreck-it tasks complete, update the corresponding external ticket status and attach the git diff URL as a comment. Build on the existing KanbanClient enum dispatch and KanbanProvider trait in cli/src/kanban/. This closes the loop between human planning tools and autonomous execution, enabling teams to manage AI workloads from their existing project management tools.

Implement dynamic budget-aware task prioritization that adjusts task execution order based on remaining cost budget, current complexity estimates, and historical cost-per-complexity ratios. Add a `--budget-strategy` CLI flag with options: `greedy` (maximize completed tasks), `critical-path` (prioritize highest-priority tasks first), and `conservative` (prefer low-complexity tasks when budget is below a threshold). Integrate with the existing `--max-cost` budget tracking to automatically pause the loop when projected remaining cost would exceed budget. This extends wreck-it's existing cost tracking into a planning optimization feature unique in the agent orchestration space.

Add an automated semantic changelog generator that runs as a recurring evaluator-role task. At the end of each iteration, it reads the provenance audit trail and completed task artefacts, groups changes by category (features, fixes, refactors, security), and generates a human-readable CHANGELOG.md entry. Use conventional-commit conventions to classify changes. Output the changelog entry as an artefact that can be consumed by release automation. This uniquely combines wreck-it's provenance audit trail, artefact system, and recurring task cooldown to create fully automated release notes with zero human effort.

Build a multi-repo change orchestration feature that detects when a completed task modifies a public API or exported interface, then automatically creates follow-up tasks in dependent repositories using the existing `--work-dir-map` multi-repo support. Implement interface change detection by comparing type signatures and exported symbols before/after the git diff. When a breaking change is detected, generate adaptation tasks in consumer repos and inject them into their respective task files. This combines wreck-it's multi-repo support and artefact system into an intelligent cross-repo dependency management capability.

Implement a fan-out/fan-in execution pattern allowing a single ideas-role task to dynamically decompose work into parallel implementer sub-tasks at runtime, then collect and synthesize their results via a fan-in aggregator task. Extend the existing `parent_id` and phase system: when an ideas task outputs a sub-task manifest artefact, the runner spawns those tasks in the next phase with the parent's context injected. The fan-in task waits for all siblings to complete and synthesizes a consolidated result. This addresses the growing trend of multi-agent coordination and enables wreck-it to tackle problems too large for a single context window.
